claim: Autonomous coding agents can deliver front-loaded velocity gains but also persistent quality degradation and technical debt.
method: Longitudinal causal study using staggered difference-in-differences with matched controls and AIDev.
unit_of_analysis: Monthly repository-level outcomes after the first agent-generated pull request in OSS repositories.
main_finding: Velocity gains are strongest when agents are the first observable AI tool, while static-analysis warnings and cognitive complexity rise persistently.
limitations: Repository-level adoption design does not identify pull-request-level mechanisms and depends on observable adoption events.
notes: Core external comparator for post-adoption debt and quality claims.

claim: Technical debt is a short-term technical compromise that raises long-term cost of change and maintainability burden.
method: Conceptual and agenda-setting seminar report.
unit_of_analysis: Technical debt concepts, management activities, and research roadmap.
main_finding: Defines TD as a management and communication concept that requires recognition, analysis, monitoring, and measurement.
limitations: Not an AI study and not based on pull-request-level repository analysis.
notes: Use for theory and definition rather than AI-specific empirical claims.

claim: Modern code review is driven by more than defect finding and depends heavily on change understanding.
method: Mixed qualitative study using observation, interviews, comment classification, and survey.
unit_of_analysis: Developers, managers, and review comments in tool-based code review at Microsoft.
main_finding: Reviews support knowledge transfer and team awareness as well as defect detection; understanding context is central.
limitations: Industrial setting rather than OSS and not AI-specific.
notes: Frames review as governance and sensemaking, not only bug finding.

claim: OSS use is pervasive and vulnerability exposure remains widespread in the AI era.
method: Industry report based on scanned codebases and governance data.
unit_of_analysis: Enterprise codebases and OSS components.
main_finding: Shows widespread OSS dependence and persistent vulnerability and governance risk, motivating why OSS quality and security matter.
limitations: Industry report; not pull-request-level, not causal, and not specific to detectable AI contributions.
notes: Use only as ecosystem context.

claim: AI-generated code snippets in real GitHub projects frequently contain security weaknesses across many CWE categories.
method: Empirical study of Copilot and other generated snippets collected from GitHub projects plus repair experiments.
unit_of_analysis: 733 generated code snippets from GitHub projects.
main_finding: Finds high rates of security weaknesses in generated Python and JavaScript snippets and partial repair success with Copilot Chat.
limitations: Snippet-level study; not pull-request-level and not about repository governance or review.
notes: Core empirical security source.

claim: Cursor adoption can raise development velocity transiently while increasing static-analysis warnings and complexity.
method: Matched difference-in-differences study.
unit_of_analysis: Project-level GitHub outcomes for Cursor-adopting and matched control projects.
main_finding: Finds significant short-term velocity gains and persistent increases in warnings and code complexity.
limitations: Project-level adoption study, not pull-request-level and not security-traceability focused.
notes: Core prior work for the velocity-quality trade-off.

theme: AIDev dataset and agentic pull requests
claim: Autonomous coding agents are already active in OSS at large scale and their pull requests show distinct acceptance and review patterns.
method: Dataset paper with descriptive analysis and case studies.
unit_of_analysis: 456,535 agentic pull requests across 61,453 repositories and 47,303 developers.
main_finding: Agentic PRs can be reviewed and merged quickly but are less likely to be accepted and tend to be structurally simpler.
limitations: Coverage begins with visible agentic PRs and does not capture hidden AI assistance.
notes: Primary external dataset and observability anchor.

claim: Code review coverage, participation, and reviewer expertise are linked to long-term software quality.
method: Empirical case study of modern code review practices and post-release defects.
unit_of_analysis: Qt, VTK, and ITK review and defect data.
main_finding: Higher review coverage, participation, and expertise are significantly linked to better software quality.
limitations: Not AI-specific and based on selected projects.
notes: Core justification for using review indicators as meaningful quality and governance signals.

theme: OSS process and case study logic
claim: Deep case studies of OSS projects can reveal coordination and quality-relevant process patterns that broad surveys miss.
method: Comparative case study.
unit_of_analysis: Apache and Mozilla OSS development processes.
main_finding: Shows how detailed case-based analysis can generate influential insights into OSS development mechanisms.
limitations: Pre-AI era and not about AI assistance directly.
notes: Methodological precedent for depth-over-breadth OSS analysis.

claim: Transformer models can identify technical debt and debt types in large-scale issue data with strong performance.
method: Machine-learning classification study with ensemble learning.
unit_of_analysis: GitHub Archive issues from 2015-2024 plus industrial validation.
main_finding: Shows that technical debt identification can be partially automated across diverse projects.
limitations: Issue-level TD identification does not directly measure patch-level debt in pull requests.
notes: Useful for TD framing and measurable debt signals.

claim: OSS supply chains operate at massive scale and vulnerability management failures create persistent security and maintenance risk.
method: Industry report and trend analysis.
unit_of_analysis: Open-source ecosystems, vulnerabilities, and supply-chain incidents.
main_finding: Documents the scale of OSS dependence, vulnerability load, and attack evolution.
limitations: Industry report rather than peer-reviewed pull-request-level evidence.
notes: Use as contextual justification for why CVE-linked traceability matters.

claim: Design debt has measurable negative effects on maintainability and defect proneness.
method: Empirical case study.
unit_of_analysis: Two software products in a small company, focusing on god classes as design debt.
main_finding: God classes are changed more often and contain more defects than non-god classes.
limitations: Small-sample case study and pre-AI context.
notes: Strong foundational debt-quality source.

claim: Shared developer-ChatGPT traces can be systematically collected and linked to software-engineering artifacts.
method: Dataset construction paper.
unit_of_analysis: Developer-ChatGPT conversations linked to code, commits, issues, pull requests, and discussions.
main_finding: Provides a reusable empirical base for studying visible LLM use in collaborative development.
limitations: Captures only conversations that are publicly shared or recoverable.
notes: Supports discourse and observability framing.

theme: Shared ChatGPT conversations in PRs and issues
claim: Developers visibly share ChatGPT conversations in GitHub pull requests and issues for multiple software-engineering tasks.
method: Empirical study with manual inspection and characterization of shared conversations.
unit_of_analysis: GitHub pull requests and issues containing shared ChatGPT conversations.
main_finding: Shows that visible ChatGPT use in collaborative artifacts is real and diverse, supporting public-discourse-based observability.
limitations: Only captures explicit shared conversations, not hidden AI use.
notes: Directly relevant to detectable AI evidence in GitHub artifacts.

theme: Agentic pull requests and human oversight
claim: Agent-assisted pull requests are often merged but still frequently require human revision and oversight.
method: Empirical study of agent-authored GitHub pull requests.
unit_of_analysis: 567 Claude Code pull requests across 157 open-source projects.
main_finding: Finds an 83.8 percent merge rate, with 54.9 percent integrated without further modification and the rest benefiting from human revisions.
limitations: Focuses on Claude Code PRs and acceptance behavior rather than long-term maintenance or security outcomes.
notes: Closest external comparator to this thesis.

theme: Review effort in AI-generated pull requests
claim: Review effort in AI-generated pull requests is a meaningful governance concern that can be predicted early.
method: Empirical study on AI-generated pull request review effort prediction.
unit_of_analysis: AI-generated pull requests and their review-effort characteristics.
main_finding: Supports the idea that AI PRs can create differentiated review workload and triage demands.
limitations: Prediction-focused and not a two-case repository study.
notes: Useful for framing review burden as a measurable phenomenon.

theme: How AI coding agents modify code
claim: Agentic pull requests differ structurally from human PRs in commits, files touched, deletions, and description-to-diff consistency.
method: Large-scale empirical pull-request study using AIDev mining-challenge data.
unit_of_analysis: 24,014 merged agentic PRs and 5,081 merged human PRs.
main_finding: Finds substantial differences in commit count and moderate differences in files touched and deleted lines; agentic PRs show slightly higher description-to-diff similarity.
limitations: Only includes merged PRs and focuses on structural differences rather than review or security outcomes.
notes: Strong comparator for patch-level metric interpretation.

claim: LLM code-generation tools can suggest insecure code at nontrivial rates.
method: Empirical security evaluation of Copilot suggestions.
unit_of_analysis: Generated code suggestions for security-sensitive programming scenarios.
main_finding: Demonstrates that AI-assisted code generation can produce vulnerable outputs, motivating security-aware analysis.
limitations: Controlled evaluation rather than repository-level field study.
notes: Foundational AI-security baseline.

claim: Large language models show promise for zero-shot vulnerability repair but struggle on realistic fixes.
method: Large-scale vulnerability repair evaluation across synthetic, hand-crafted, and real-world scenarios.
unit_of_analysis: Security bug scenarios and generated repair attempts.
main_finding: Models can repair many synthetic or hand-crafted cases, but real-world functional correctness remains difficult.
limitations: Repair study, not pull-request-level field evidence.
notes: Useful to nuance the security discussion beyond vulnerability creation.

theme: Technical debt in the generative AI era
claim: Technical debt management must adapt to AI-enhanced development and AI-driven systems.
method: Multivocal literature review.
unit_of_analysis: Technical debt literature spanning DevOps to generative AI.
main_finding: Identifies new debt challenges around maintainability, explainability, and data and governance in AI-enhanced software engineering.
limitations: Review paper rather than repository-level empirical analysis.
notes: Best bridge between TD theory and the GenAI era.

claim: A proper software-engineering SLR must use a predefined protocol, search strategy, inclusion and exclusion rules, extraction, and reporting.
method: Method guideline technical report.
unit_of_analysis: Systematic literature review process in software engineering.
main_finding: Defines planning, conducting, and reporting phases and emphasizes auditability and repeatability.
limitations: High-level guidance rather than a topic-specific study.
notes: Primary methodological anchor if the thesis uses SLR language.

claim: Snowballing can be a rigorous complement to database searches in software-engineering literature studies.
method: Method guideline and replication study.
unit_of_analysis: Systematic literature search process.
main_finding: Provides a defensible process for backward and forward snowballing.
limitations: Method paper rather than substantive domain evidence.
notes: Use to justify citation chaining from seed papers.

claim: Case study research in software engineering can be rigorous when design, selection, data collection, and validity threats are explicit.
method: Method guideline journal article.
unit_of_analysis: Software-engineering case study design and reporting.
main_finding: Provides established guidance for designing and reporting case studies in software engineering.
limitations: Not specific to AI or OSS.
notes: Primary case-study methodology anchor.

claim: Mixed-methods research combines quantitative and qualitative evidence to answer questions neither could resolve alone.
method: Research-methods book.
unit_of_analysis: Mixed-methods design logic and integration strategies.
main_finding: Provides design logic for combining quantitative comparison with qualitative interpretation.
limitations: Book source rather than empirical software-engineering study.
notes: Use to justify the mixed-methods integration logic.

claim: Early-2025 AI tools did not speed up experienced open-source contributors in realistic tasks and may slow them down.
method: Randomized controlled trial.
unit_of_analysis: 246 tasks by 16 experienced OSS developers working on mature projects they knew well.
main_finding: Allowing AI increased completion time by 19 percent, contrary to participant and expert expectations.
limitations: Experienced contributors on familiar projects may not generalize to all developers or tasks.
notes: Important counterpoint to broad AI productivity claims.

claim: Current LLM-based code-review systems underperform on full pull-request review and need stronger benchmarks and aggregation strategies.
method: Benchmark and evaluation study.
unit_of_analysis: 1000 GitHub pull requests in SWRBench with full project context.
main_finding: Introduces SWRBench and finds current automated review systems underperform, while multi-review aggregation improves results.
limitations: Focuses on automated review systems, not AI-authored pull requests.
notes: Secondary context for AI review tooling rather than core evidence.
